bio.bio_blurb: First wife of Jonathan Edwards Jr.; drowned 1782

narrative_blocks.body: Riding into the country, she stopped at a New Haven mill-pond to let her horse drink; it waded beyond its depth and both went under. Her stone in the Center Church crypt is the only one there to name a cause of death — “accidentally drowned.”

narrative_blocks.body: Riding into the country, she stopped at a New Haven mill-pond to let her horse drink; it waded beyond its depth and both went under. Her stone in the Center Church crypt is the only one there to name a cause of death — “accidentally drowned.”

sources.short: Edward Hooker 1909

narrative_blocks.body: In 1773 he published antislavery articles in the Connecticut Journal under the pseudonym 'Antidoulios' — Greek for 'against slavery.' His 1791 sermon 'The Injustice and Impolicy of the Slave Trade' delivered antislavery from his New Haven pulpit.

narrative_blocks.header: Mohican linguist — Washington thanked him personally
narrative_blocks.body: He grew up in Stockbridge speaking Mohican before English, writing that 'it became more familiar to me than my mother tongue.' His 1788 'Observations on the Language of the Muhhekaneew Indians' was acknowledged by George Washington and circulated through Madison to Jefferson.

narrative_blocks.header: His first wife drowned at New Haven 1782
narrative_blocks.body: His first wife Mary Porter died June 10, 1782 in New Haven by drowning, leaving four children. He remarried in December 1783 to Mary Sabins, with whom he had no further children.

narrative_blocks.header: Died as president of Union College, 1801
narrative_blocks.body: In May 1799 he was chosen President of Union College in Schenectady, New York. He served less than two years, dying in office August 1, 1801, age 56 — leaving the abolitionist Edwards line for his sons to carry.

Ordained only four years, the twenty-eight-year-old pastor took a pen name for his 1773 newspaper attack on slavery: Antidoulios, Greek for “against slavery.” He applied the golden rule directly — why are slaveholders exempt from “whatsoever ye would that men should do to you”?

education.notes: ranked first in a class of thirty-one

career.organization: White Haven (2nd Congregational) Church, New Haven

notable.notable_blurb: Litchfield Female Academy 1794; NPG portrait at Yale

narrative_blocks.header: She and her future husband shared Litchfield
narrative_blocks.detail: Elizabeth's portrait, painted by Gerritt Schipper in 1805, now hangs at the Yale University Art Gallery. She and Jonathan had eleven children, six of whom graduated from Yale — a record that prompted the Yale obituary of one son to call them 'a family of six brothers, all graduates of this college.' She was the daughter of Moses Tryon, a family name that echoes through her children: Tryon Edwards (HD2632, the theologian) was named directly for her family. She died in 1857, the year Abraham Lincoln began his presidential campaign, having lived through the entire Early Republic. (Litchfield Historical Society; NPG Catalog of American Portraits)
narrative_blocks.body: She attended Sarah Pierce’s Female Academy in 1794, the same year Jonathan Walter Edwards was studying at the Law School up the street — they married three years later and raised ten children. The 1909 genealogy miscopied her surname as “Lyon.”

notable.notable_blurb: Pastor who wrote 'A Dictionary of Thoughts'

narrative_blocks.header: He compiled a famous dictionary of quotations
narrative_blocks.body: Tryon Edwards assembled A Dictionary of Thoughts, a much-reprinted compendium of quotations that long outlived him. He is often credited with the maxim that between two evils one should choose neither, and between two goods, both.

A Congregational pastor in Rochester and New London, Tryon Edwards was a great-grandson of Jonathan Edwards. He edited works drawn from the Edwards manuscripts, including a volume on Christian charity.

cross_connections.display_label: Cousin raised together; Burr lodged at her Savannah home

narrative_blocks.header: Aaron Burr negotiated her children's return from England
narrative_blocks.body: Her first marriage at about fifteen to Sir Charles Rupert Dennis of England was disastrous; he treated her cruelly and they divorced. He abducted their children to England (13 Harley Street London) — her first cousin Aaron Burr Jr, with whom she had been raised, assisted in negotiating the children's return.

narrative_blocks.header: Son organized illegal slave ship Wanderer, 1858
narrative_blocks.body: Her son John Samuel de Montmollin II organized the illegal slave ship Wanderer in 1858 — a Charleston yacht refitted to smuggle 409 enslaved Africans into Georgia after the international slave trade had been banned for fifty years. Her own father Jonathan Edwards Jr had been among America's first abolitionists.

narrative_blocks.header: Aaron Burr lodged at her Savannah home, 1802
narrative_blocks.body: Vice President Aaron Burr — her first cousin and childhood housemate — lodged at the DeMontmollin house at 120 Oglethorpe Street Savannah in 1802 and again in 1807. The visits came during Burr's national political prominence and his subsequent flight from Eastern political circles after the Hamilton duel.

narrative_blocks.header: Buried under maiden name Edwards to honor parents
narrative_blocks.body: She died 1 September 1859 at Savannah Georgia and was buried at Laurel Grove Cemetery under the surname 'EDWARDS' — her birth name — chosen at death to honor her parents' memory. Her father Jonathan Edwards Jr and mother Mary Porter had both died when she was a young girl.

narrative_blocks.header: Abolitionist's grandson became a slave trader
narrative_blocks.body: His grandfather Jonathan Edwards Jr. was a Connecticut minister who preached against slavery in the 1790s. By the 1850s, John was brokering enslaved people in Savannah and operating a slave pen above his warehouse.

narrative_blocks.header: Sent the schooner Wanderer to the Congo
narrative_blocks.body: In 1858 his ship made an illegal voyage to West Africa and returned with four hundred captives delivered to coastal Georgia plantations. A federal grand jury declined to indict him in April 1859.

narrative_blocks.header: His slave pen became Savannah's first Black school
narrative_blocks.body: After Sherman took Savannah in December 1864, the U.S. government gave the warehouse to the city's African American community. On January 10, 1865, Black children marched from First African Baptist Church to the building and opened Bryan School there.

narrative_blocks.header: Killed in steamboat blast near his slave pen
narrative_blocks.body: On June 9, 1859 the J.G. Lawton's boiler exploded fourteen miles up the Savannah River. His body was found seventy yards away in the marsh, a handkerchief still gripped in his hand.

narrative_blocks.header: Will demanded education, sobriety, two foreign languages
narrative_blocks.body: He instructed his children to conduct themselves as Christians, learn French and Spanish fluently, and avoid liquor and tobacco. The will took ten years to settle in court.
